segments.s076-g25: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' intensifying the following predicate. شاد شد is a simple past (perfective) verbal phrase 'became happy' (شد = become). زان سخن is a prepositional phrase ('زان' = 'from/at that' + 'سخن' = 'speech/words') giving the cause. پهلوان appears after the verb as the subject (postverbal subject is common in poetic Persian), so the literal order is 'so happy became from that speech the champion'.
segments.s076-g26: که introduces a result clause ('that/so that'). با marks accompaniment ('with/in the company of'). پیر is 'old man/elder'. سر here is a predicative noun 'head/person' attached to the verb شد (literary phrasing: 'سر شد' = 'became' in a state concerning person/head) and combines with the following phrase. شد is the past 'became'. به + نوی + جوان is a directional/result phrase 'into new youth' (به = into/towards, نوی = new, جوان = young), so the clause says that, in the elder's company, he 'became into new youth' — i.e. was made young again.
segments.s076-g27: Literal word order is noun (سواری) + preposition (به) + place (کابل) + verb (برافگند) + adverb (زود). برافگند is a past-tense poetic verb meaning essentially 'sent off' or 'flung away/dispersed' here used transitively ('sent a rider'); زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying that action.
segments.s076-g28: به مهراب گفت is 'said to Mehrab' (به marks the indirect object). آن introduces the reported interrogative; کجا رفته بود is past perfect ('had gone/been gone'). گفت is the past reporting verb; the clause as a whole is reported speech: 'he said to Mehrab where he had gone.'
segments.s076-g29: نوازیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'to please/entertain/flatter' and takes شهریار جهان ('the king/sovereign of the world') as its object; جهان is in apposition to شهریار ('sovereign of the world'). The phrase functions as a purpose/object phrase.
segments.s076-g30: وزان = و + زان ('and from that'); شادمانی is 'joy/rejoicing'. که introduces a relative clause modifying شادمانی; رفت is simple past 'went/vanished' and از مهان ('from the guests') is an ablative phrase showing source. Together: 'and from that joy which had left the guests.'
segments.s076-g31: من اینک ('I now') sets the speaker and time. چو here means 'when'; دستان is used in the sense 'tales/stories' (classical Persian uses دستان for narratives). بر من رسد is a verbal clause 'reach/come upon me' with رسد (subjunctive/present form) meaning 'reach' or 'happen to.'
segments.s076-g32: گذاریم is a first-person plural present/subjunctive form used here to express 'I/we will put/present/leave' (classical/polite plural usage is common). هر دو 'both/either', چنان چون sزد = 'in such a way as (چون) befits (سزد)'; سزد is impersonal/present 'it befits.' The whole means 'I (or we, politely) will set/relate both as is fitting.'
segments.s076-g33: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' and modifies the following predicate. شاد شد is a compound predicate: شاد (adjective 'happy') + شد (past of شدن 'to become'), so 'became happy'. شاه کابلستان is the subject (title + place-name) placed after the verb, a common order in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s076-g34: ز introduces a cause/ source here ('because of' / 'from'). پیوند is a noun 'bond/union' (here: marriage/union). خورشید زابلستان is a genitive group where خورشید (a proper name derived from 'sun') is qualified by زابلستان ('of Zabulistan'). The whole phrase gives the reason for the king's joy.
segments.s076-g35: که introduces a subordinate/relative or explanatory clause ('that' / 'as'), and گفتی here functions as a nominal/quotational phrase ('what was said' / 'as is said') rather than the simple 2nd‑person verb. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here intensifying the action). جان برافشاندند is a verb phrase: جان (life) + برافشاندند (past plural 'they scattered/let fly'), a poetic way to say they poured out or risked their lives.
segments.s076-g36: ز هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'from every place' (ز + هر + جای). رامشگران is the plural agent noun 'peacemakers/comforters' and is the subject of خواندند, a past plural verb which here can mean 'sang/uttered/called' depending on context. The clause thus describes people from every place making a vocal response.
segments.s076-g37: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. مهراب is the subject (a proper name in the Shahnameh) and شد is the past 'became'. شاد و روشن‌روان are predicate adjectives linked by و ('happy and bright‑souled/cheerful'), describing the state he assumed.
segments.s076-g38: لبش ('his lip' or more idiomatically 'his expression') is the grammatical subject of گشت خندان ('became smiling'); گشت is another past form of شدن 'to become/turn', and خندان is the resulting adjective 'smiling'. The conjunction و links a second nominal phrase دل شادمان ('(his) heart (became) rejoicing') in which the copula is omitted but implied.
segments.s076-g39: گرانمایه is an epithet 'precious/esteemed' modifying the proper name سیندخت (Sindukht). را is the definite object marker attached to سیندخت, and پیش خواند is a verbal phrase 'called before (him)' — خواند here means 'summoned/called' and پیش indicates 'into his presence'.
segments.s076-g40: بسی is an adverb intensifier ('much/very' in classical usage) modifying خوب‌گفتار ('eloquent' / 'good‑spoken'). با او is a prepositional phrase 'with her/to her', and براند (past) is from راندن 'to drive/lead/escort' used here in the sense 'he led/treated/spoke to her' — the verb continues the action of the subject from the previous clause (the king).
segments.s076-g17: گسی (a certain person / someone) is the direct object being referred to; کردمش is a past‑tense 1st‑person verbal form meaning 'I made/caused (him to go)' or idiomatically 'I sent him off' (the enclitic -مش marks the object). با دلی شادمان is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a joyful heart' (با + noun + adjective), placed after the verb to describe the speaker's state while sending him.
segments.s076-g18: کزو is a compound of کِ + زُ (poetic 'from which / from whom') introducing the source or thing to be kept distant; دور بادا is an optative formula ('may ... be far') where بادا is the classical optative particle 'may it be', and بد بدگمان is a doubled descriptive phrase 'bad / ill suspicion' (بد used as intensifier + بدگمان 'ill‑thinking, suspicion'). The whole clause is a wish: 'may ill suspicion be far from him.'
segments.s076-g19: برون‌رفت here functions as a verbal idea 'came out / appeared' (a classical verbal noun used verbally in poetry). با introduces accompaniment 'with Farrokh (فرخی) and Zal (زال)' and زر is the theme 'gold' that accompanies the movement. Word order is poetic: circumstantial verb/nominal verb phrase + accompaniment + object.
segments.s076-g20: ز گردان لشکر literally 'from the rotating/turned ranks of the army' — ز is the poetic preposition 'from', گردان لشکر is a genitive chain 'the troop/division of the host', and برآورده سر is a participial/poetic phrase meaning 'heads having been raised' (سر برآوردن = 'to lift/raise the head' or 'to show themselves'). The clause describes people (heads) emerging from the army's ranks.
segments.s076-g21: نوندی is the name of a musical mode or tune (Navand) used as a noun; برافگند is a past tense/preterite verb meaning 'they cast/struck/sounded' (here 'sounded a tune'). نزدیک سام is an adverbial phrase 'near Sam' (proper name placed after نزدیک). The clause describes a musical action performed in Sam's vicinity.
segments.s076-g22: که introduces the following explanatory clause ('for / that / because'); برگشتم is 1st‑person past 'I returned'; از شاه 'from the king' gives the origin or occasion of the return; دل شادکام is a predicate phrase 'with a glad/fortunate heart' (دل + adjective). The whole clause explains the reason for the celebration: 'because I returned from the king, heart joyful.'
segments.s076-g23: ابا is an archaic verb meaning 'to refuse / to be reluctant (to accept)' and governs the following objects; خلعت خسروانی و تاج is a coordinated noun phrase 'the robe of kingship (robe of honor) and the crown'. Classical Persian often places the verb before its objects in poetic lines, and ابا here negates acceptance of the listed royal gifts.
segments.s076-g24: همان yاره و طوق و هم تخت عاج continues the list of rejected gifts: همان 'that/same' qualifies یاره (a poetic term often used for a treasured companion or prized thing), followed by و طوق 'and the collar/necklace', and هم تخت عاج 'and also the ivory throne' (هم 'also/even' + تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory'). The coordination links multiple objects refused by the subject implied earlier.
segments.s076-g81: همه modifies کابلستان here as a determiner meaning 'the whole/entire' (word order: determiner + place). شد is the past 3rd-person singular of شدن 'to become' and makes آراسته a predicate adjective: 'became adorned.' آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned' linked to کابلستان by شد.
segments.s076-g82: پر از is an idiomatic phrase 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)' + از = 'of'); رنگ و بوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'color and scent.' The repeated پر از ... parallelizes the first phrase with خواسته ('wish/longing'), so the second از again marks the complement of 'full of.'
segments.s076-g83: همه here means 'everyone' (subject). پشت پیلان is a genitive group 'the backs of the elephants' (پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephant'). بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural of بیاراستن 'to adorn/ornament,' agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s076-g84: ز کابل is a prepositional phrase 'from Kabul' (ز = poetic 'from'). پرستندگان is the subject or agent 'worshippers/devotees.' خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural of خواستن and here means 'they requested/wished' (past tense). Word order (preposition + place before subject + verb) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s076-g85: نشستند is the past 3rd-person plural of نشستن 'to sit/seat' ('they sat'). In this poetic line the noun رامشگران ('entertainers/comforters/peacemakers') comes after the verb, so the phrase reads 'the رامشگران sat on the elephants' (بر = 'on, upon'; پیل = 'elephant').
segments.s076-g86: نهاده is a past-participle 'having placed/put' used attributively; it governs the following locative phrases. به سر means 'on the head' (به used in the sense 'to/at/on' with سر 'head'), بر here again 'on/upon' introduces the phrase زر افسران where زر 'gold' qualifies افسران 'helmets' (زر افسران = 'golden helmets'). The whole segment describes that golden helmets were placed on (their) heads.
segments.s076-g87: پذیره شدن is a nominalized verb phrase 'the reception/being received' (پذیره = reception, شدن = 'to become' used here to form the event). را is the definite object marker marking this phrase as the object, and بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they adorned/arranged' — 'they prepared/adorned the reception.'
segments.s076-g88: نثارش is 'an offering to him/her' (نثار + pronominal clitic ش). همه here means 'everyone' as the agent of خواستند. مشک و زر are the coordinated items 'musk and gold.' خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural of خواستن 'to want/wish' and here means 'desired/wished (to offer).' Word order places the object (نثارش) before the subject (همه) and verb for poetic effect.
segments.s076-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is a past‑tense 3rd‑person verb 'heard' placed before the subject (poetic inversion). سیندخت is the subject (proper name). ازو is the prepositional pronoun 'from him/it' referring back to the previously mentioned person. گشت باز is a compound: گشت (turned/ became) + باز (back/again) = 'turned back.'
segments.s076-g50: بر + دختر آمد = 'came toward the maiden' (بر = toward, آمد = came). سراینده راز is an appositive/description: سراینده is an agentive noun 'the one who sang/told' and راز specifies what was told — 'the teller (singer) of the secret.' Word order places the descriptive noun phrase after the verb phrase in poetic style.
segments.s076-g51: همی is an adverb signaling continued or habitual action ('kept/continually'). مژده is 'good news' or 'tidings'. دادش is the past verb داد 'gave' with the enclitic object -ش ('her' or 'him') — here 'gave her (the news)'. به دیدار زال = 'about/ of the meeting with Zal' (به marks the relation; دیدار = meeting; زال = the name).
segments.s076-g52: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / introducing the reported question). دیدی here is 2nd‑person past 'have you seen' (used as an interrogative). چنان 'such' and چون 'as' form the comparative clause 'such as'. بباید is a modal 'ought/could/should be' (poetic older form). همال is an archaic word meaning 'a match/peer' (here 'one equal to him'); it functions as the complement of بباید.
segments.s076-g53: زن و مرد = 'woman and man' (a paired nominal). را is the definite/object/topic marker attaching to that phrase. از + بلندی منش = 'from/of lofty origin' or 'by high birth/standing' (از marks relation; بلندی منش = 'height/loftiness of origin' = noble birth or high character). The whole segment sets the class ('women and men of high origin') to which the following statement applies.
segments.s076-g54: سزدگر is an agentive/impersonal form 'one who deems it fitting' or more loosely 'it is fitting/worthy that'. فرازد is past‑tense 3rd‑person 'he raised/exalted' (here 'raise up'). سر = 'head' (literally 'head' = honor/pride) and از سرزنش = 'from reproach' (از = from; سرزنش = reproach). Together: 'it is fitting that the worthy one lift his head out of reproach.'
segments.s076-g55: سوی = 'toward'. کام دل is a genitive phrase 'the desire of the heart' (کام = desire, دل = heart). تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift' or 'keen'. بشتافتی is 2nd‑person past 'you hastened/rushed' (poetic past form), so the whole means 'you hastened quickly toward your heart's desire.'
segments.s076-g56: کنون = 'now'. هر چه = 'whatever/whatever thing'. جستی is past 2nd‑person of 'جست' here meaning 'you sought/sought for' (not 'leapt'). همه = 'all' or 'entirely'. یافتی is past 2nd‑person of یافت 'you found/obtained'. Together: 'Now whatever you sought, you have all obtained.'
segments.s076-g1: پس is a temporal connective 'then'. آن نامه is the definite object 'that letter' placed before the verb (typical Persian object–verb order for emphasis). سام is the subject (proper name). پاسخ نوشت is a compound verb phrase: پاسخ (answer) as the direct object + نوشت (past simple 'wrote').
segments.s076-g2: شگفتی here qualifies the following noun phrase as 'marvellous/wonderful'; سخنهای is the plural of 'speech/words' (construct form 'words of ...'); فرخ functions adjectivally as 'fortunate/auspicious' modifying سخنها; نوشت repeats the verb 'wrote' (poetic parallelism). The whole segment means he 'wrote wondrous, auspicious words.'
segments.s076-g3: که‌ای is a poetic vocative (که + ای) addressing someone 'O...'; نامور پهلوان دلیر is a noun phrase with two adjectival modifiers: نامور 'renowned' and دلیر 'brave' modifying پهلوان 'champion/hero'. Word order places adjectives around the noun for emphasis.
segments.s076-g4: به هر کار پیروز is an adjectival/participial phrase 'victorious in every deed' where به + noun marks the domain ('in/at every work'); برسان is an imperative/optative form of رساندن (poetically without final -د) meaning 'bring' or 'may (he/you) bring'; شیر is the direct object 'the lion' (here likely metaphorical).
segments.s076-g5: نبیند is a negative present/poetic perfective sense 'does not see' or more idiomatically 'there is not seen'; چو تو 'like you' is a comparative clause (چو = 'as/like'); نیز 'also'/'too' emphasizes inclusion; گردان سپهر is a poetic epithet 'the turning/revolving sky' (گردان modifying سپهر). The phrase says that the heavens see none like you.
segments.s076-g6: A parallel list introduced by repeated به ('in/at/for'): به رزم 'in battle', به بزم 'in the banquet/feast', به رای 'in counsel/opinion' and به چهر 'in face/appearance'. The repetition of به and و (and) creates an enumerative, poetic rhythm; each noun names a sphere in which the addressee excels.
segments.s076-g7: همان 'that very' points back; پور is 'son' used as a title/identifier; فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' modifies پور; زال is the proper name (here in genitive relation) and سوار 'rider/horseman' forms the appositive 'Zal's rider' or 'the rider son of Zal'. The phrase is a compact epitheton identifying the hero.
segments.s076-g8: کزو = از + که (from whom/of whom) introduces the source; ماند (past 'remained/was left') + اندر 'in' + جهان 'the world' + یادگار 'memorial/remembrance' means 'a memorial/remembrance remained of him in the world.' The verb ماند is simple past and yields the resulting state.
segments.s076-g9: رسید is a third-person past verb 'arrived/come' with an implied subject; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; بدانستم is first-person past 'I knew/understood'. از کام او is a prepositional phrase: از 'from' marks source, کام is 'desire/wish', and او is the third‑person possessive pronoun 'his/her' referring back to the person just mentioned.
segments.s076-g10: همان is a demonstrative 'that very/the same' modifying the following nouns; خواهش و رای و آرام is a coordinated list 'desire (or request), counsel/opinion, and calm/tranquillity'; او is the possessive 'his' attached to the final noun but applying to the whole list. Word order places the demonstrative before the coordinated nouns, as usual in poetry.
segments.s076-g11: برآمد is a past/past‑result verb 'arose/occurred/came to pass'. هر آنچ 'هر' + 'آنچ' = 'whatever/whatever that which' (a free relative). آن ترا is a classical sequence where آن 'that' combines with the enclitic ترا (the old dative/accusative 'to/for you') — together meaning 'that which was to you/that pleased you'. کام بود is the copula 'was (a) desire' (کام = 'desire', بود = past 'was'), so the whole clause means 'whatever that was your desire came about.'
segments.s076-g12: همان 'that same' is the demonstrative head; زال را places Zal as the topical/affected person (را here topical/emphatic rather than simple object marking). رای و آرام بود is the predicate 'there was counsel/opinion and calm' (رای = 'plan/counsel', آرام = 'calm/quiet', بود = past copula 'was'). Together: 'That same counsel and calm were Zal's.'
segments.s076-g13: همه آرزوها is the direct object 'all the wishes'; سپردم is first‑person past 'I entrusted/handed over'. بدوی here is an archaic/dialectal dative meaning 'to him/to that one' (classical spelling of به + دوی); this sense is not among the provided sense options, so it is noted as -1. The clause thus means 'I entrusted all the wishes to him.'
segments.s076-g14: بسی روز فرخ شمردم is 'I counted many/ numerous fortunate (happy) days' with بسی functioning as a quantifier 'many' (here used in the sense 'many/much'), روز 'days', فرخ 'fortunate/happy', and شمردم 'I counted'. As in the previous segment, the final بدوی repeats the archaic dative 'to him/for him' (not represented in the provided sense list, hence -1).
segments.s076-g15: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; شیری is the leonine adjective/noun 'lion(-like)' here referring to 'a lion'; که باشد introduces a relative clause 'that is/that may be' with باشد in a general/subjunctive sense; شکارش is 'its prey/quarry' (شکار + possessive suffix -ش referring back to the lion); پلنگ names the quarry 'leopard'. The clause literally: 'From a lion whose quarry is a leopard...' (i.e. if a leopard is the lion's prey).
segments.s076-g16: چه is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what' used rhetorically; زاید (from the root زاد) means 'produces/begets/comes forth'; جز از ... 'except from ...' sets the only possible result; شیر شرزه is 'a lion that is ferocious/insolent/war‑like' (شیر = 'lion', شرزه = 'rebellious/ferocious'); به جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'in/for battle' (به + جنگ). Together: 'What will be born except, from such a lion, one fierce in battle?'
segments.s076-g73: همه پیکرش is a noun phrase meaning “her whole body” with همه as a determiner (‘whole/entire’) and the possessive enclitic -ش on پیکر. گوهر آگنده is a predicative phrase: گوهر (gems) + آگنده (laden/full of) describes the state, and بود is the past copula (simple past 'was').
segments.s076-g74: میان introduces a locative phrase 'among/amongst'; گهر نقشها is the noun phrase 'gems (amongst) the designs/patterns' and کنده is a past participle meaning 'carved' (passive/stative) followed by بود as the past auxiliary — together 'were carved' or 'had been carved.' The construction is stative/passive in tone, typical of epic diction.
segments.s076-g75: ز یاقوت is a prepositional phrase 'of/from ruby' qualifying پایه. مر is an archaic/dialectal dative/clitic meaning 'for me' placed after یاقوت. تخت را پایه بود places تخت را (the throne — definite marked object/topic) before the predicate پایه بود: 'there was a pedestal (for the throne) of ruby (for me).' بود is the past copula.
segments.s076-g76: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'which/that': تخت کیان بود is a predication 'it was the throne of the Kians' (کیان = the royal house/kings). The clause continues with و joining a second predicate پرمایه بود ('and was richly endowed/substantial'), with بود repeated as copula for each predicate — a common poetic pattern.
segments.s076-g77: یک ایوان is the head noun 'an iwan/porch.' همه جامه is a grouping 'all (kinds of) garments/robes' placed before the list of items. رود here appears as an item paired with می (wine); the exact sense of رود in this context is not represented in the provided senses (so treated as unknown). می is the noun 'wine.' The verb completing the clause appears in the next segment (بیاورده).
segments.s076-g78: بیاورده is the past participle (used here as a past action) meaning 'were brought' or 'they brought,' and از introduces the source list 'from Pars, Ahvaz, and Rey.' The cities are given in a coordinative sequence linked by و. Word order is the typical postpositional source phrase after the verb.
segments.s076-g79: بیاراست is a past form of آراستن 'to adorn' (third person past), appearing before its object in this poetic order; رودابه را is the definite object marked by را. چون نگار is a simile 'like a beauty/beloved' describing how she was adorned.
segments.s076-g80: پر از X is an idiomatic phrase 'full of X': پر 'full' + از introduces the list of items (جامه، رنگ، بوی). Items are coordinated with و. بوی بهار is a genitive construction 'the scent of spring.' The whole phrase modifies how Rudabeh was adorned.
segments.s076-g41: بدو گفت = به + او (to her) + گفت (said) in classical word order: the indirect object (بدو) precedes the speech verb. کای is a vocative particle ("O"). جفت فرخنده رای is a nominal phrase in which جفت (pair) is modified by فرخنده (auspicious) and رای (counsel/opinion); together it functions as the form of address. Note: گفت is the speech verb 'said' (classical past), though the supplied lemma senses did not list that verbal sense.
segments.s076-g42: بیفروخت is a past negative/denial meaning 'did not sell' or 'refused to part with' (3rd person). از is the preposition 'from'. رایت is the banner/standard, and این تیره جای is the noun phrase 'this dark/ominous place' (این = this; تیره = dark/ominous; جای = place/seat). The whole phrase says that he would not give up this gloomy place from (under) his standard.
segments.s076-g43: به is a preposition here ('at/to/with'). شاخی is the noun 'branch' (classical adjective/noun form), and زدی is the 2nd-person past 'you struck' (زدن). دست ('hand') is the instrument or direct object of the action. کاندر is an archaic relative/adverbial 'which/wherein; in which' and زمین is 'ground/earth' — so the clause means you struck (with) your hand at a branch that was in the ground.
segments.s076-g44: برو is the imperative 'go'. The following clause شهریاران کنند آفرین is a subordinate/result clause: شهریاران (kings) is the subject, کنند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of 'to do/make', and آفرین is 'acclamation'/'bravo' (the thing they will 'make'); together: 'kings will make acclamation' or 'kings will praise you.'
segments.s076-g45: چنان هم = 'so/thus' with هم adding emphasis or 'indeed'. کجا is the interrogative 'where'. ساختی is 2nd-person past 'you made/constructed'. از نخست is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'from the first' (از = from, نخست = first/initially). The clause asks where/how something was made in that manner originally.
segments.s076-g46: بیاید here has a modal/subjunctive force 'may come' / 'let it come' (or 'it be fitting that it come'), followed by the dative pronoun مر ('to me'). این را is the object marker + 'this'. سرانجام is 'at last/in the end'. جست is past of a verb meaning 'sought' (here 'sought/desired'). Together the clause expresses the idea that, ultimately, he sought that this should come to me (i.e. he aimed for this outcome to reach me).
segments.s076-g47: همه is the universal quantifier 'all'. گنج is 'treasure'. پیش تو = 'before you / in your presence' (preposition + pronoun). آراستست is a classical stative verb 'is adorned / is arranged' (3rd singular, archaic -ست ending). The clause states that all the treasure has been arranged before you.
segments.s076-g48: اگر is the conditional 'if'. تخت is 'throne'. عاجست = 'is (made of) ivory' (adjectival copula structure: تخت عاج: 'an ivory throne' + -ست 'is'). The second اگر + خواستست repeats the conditional/emphatic sense: خواستست is an archaic/past-form of 'to want/wish' (here functioning as a conditional 'if you wish' / 'if it is desired'). The whole line offers the ivory throne as available, if desired.
segments.s076-g57: بدو (to him) is a prepositional pronoun placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’ (here used in narrative) and the speaker (رودابه‌ای شاه زن) follows it — a common poetic inversion. رودابه‌ای is the proper name Rudabeh with the -ای ending used in classical Persian to mark the name/nominal phrase; شاه زن is a compound meaning ‘royal woman’ (literal ‘king/woman’), attributive to رودابه.
segments.s076-g58: سزای ستایش is a noun-phrase in construct (ʼezâfe implied): سزای = ‘the desert/recompense/that which is deserved’ of ستایش ‘praise’; به هر انجمن is a prepositional phrase ‘in/at every assembly’ modifying the predicate. Altogether: ‘deserving of praise in every gathering.’
segments.s076-g59: من (I) is the subject; از خاک پای تو is a humble genitive phrase ‘from the dust of your feet’ (از = ‘from’ + خاک = ‘dust’ + پای تو = ‘your feet’); بالین کنم uses بالین ‘pillow’ as the object and کنَم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive used here as a volitional/future-like ‘I will make/lay’. Word order places the object before the verb, conventional in Persian.
segments.s076-g60: به فرمانت is a prepositional phrase ‘by/at your command’ (به + فرمان + enclitic -ت ‘your’), which modifies the following verb phrase. آرایش دین is a noun phrase ‘the adornment/arrangement of religion’ (آرایشِ دین, ezâfe implied) and کنَم (1st-person subjunctive) completes the verbal idea ‘I will arrange/do’. The subjunctive here expresses volition/intent.
segments.s076-g61: ز (poetic variant of از) تو = ‘from you’; چشم آهرمنان = ‘the eyes of the devils’ (چشم + plural آهرمنان in genitive relation), and دور باد is an optative formula ‘may [it] be far’. باد here is not ‘wind’ but the optative particle meaning ‘may it be’. The clause thus prays that the devils’ gaze be kept away from you.
segments.s076-g62: دل و جان تو = ‘your heart and your life/soul’ (possessive -ت attaches to جان but governs both coordinated nouns; ezâfe implied). خانه سور = ‘a house/home of feasting’ (دو‑noun compound), and باد again is the optative particle ‘may it be’. The whole is a blessing: ‘May your heart and life be a house of feast.’
segments.s076-g63: چو = ‘when’; بشنید is the past tense ‘(she) heard’; سیندخت is the subject (proper name). گفتار اوی is the object phrase ‘his speech/words’ with اوی the enclitic possessive ‘his’. Note the poetic order: verb before subject is acceptable in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s076-g64: به آرایش کاخ = ‘to/for the adornment of the palace’ (به + آرایشِ کاخ, ezâfe implied). بنهاد is the past-tense verb ‘he/she set/placed’ or idiomatically ‘set (her) face to/turned to’ and روی here functions adverbially ‘(her) face/toward’ (روی بنهاد = ‘she turned her face toward’). Together: she set about adorning the palace.
segments.s076-g65: بیاراست is a past tense verb (3rd‑person singular) 'adorned/arranged' placed before its object in elevated/poetic word order; ایوانها is the plural object 'the iwans/halls', and چون بهشت is a simile 'like paradise' (چون = 'like/as', بهشت = 'paradise').
segments.s076-g66: A list of nouns joined by و ('and') ends with the verb سرشت. The final verb سرشت is past 'fashioned/created' and governs the preceding coordinated nouns as its objects or materials; the coordination (گلاب و می‌و مشک و عنبر) should be read as items produced or arranged.
segments.s076-g67: بساطی is an indefinite noun 'a carpet' (‑ی gives an indefinite/partitive sense); بیفگند is past 'she/he laid/spread' (bi‑fagand, archaic/past form), so the clause reads 'she spread a carpet'; پیکر به زر is a noun phrase 'a figure/body in/with gold' where به marks material/instrument ('in/with gold').
segments.s076-g68: زبر جد here is the poetically split form of the gem name زبرجد (topaz) split for meter; برو functions as a small archaic/prepositional element linked with بافته ('woven') to mean 'woven on/through', بافته is a past participle 'woven', and سر به سر is an idiom 'from end to end / all the way through'. The clause thus says the topaz was woven throughout the fabric. (Several short poetic forms are metrically split.)
segments.s076-g69: دگر here means 'another / the rest', marking a different part; پیکرش is the noun پیکر 'body' with the 3rd‑person possessive suffix ‑ش 'her', so 'her body'; در خوشاب بود is a locative clause 'was in خوشاب' (خوشاب = 'pleasant water/sweet water'), with بود as the past copula 'was'.
segments.s076-g70: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/so that / such that'; هر دانه‌ای 'each bead/pearl' uses the indefinite marker ‑ای; قطره‌ای آب بود is 'was a drop of water' (قطره‌ای = 'a drop', آب = 'water') with بود as past 'was'—a descriptive simile: every bead was a drop.
segments.s076-g71: This clause begins with the location noun ایوان 'iwan/porch' implied by 'one iwan' (یک is understood from the line context), همه تخت زرین نهاد: همه modifies تخت as 'the whole/entire throne'; زرین is the adjective 'golden'; نهاد is the past tense 'placed/put' (3rd‑person singular).
segments.s076-g72: به + آیین و آرایش gives a circumstantial phrase 'with ceremony and with adornment' (به marking manner/instrument); چین نهاد repeats the verb نهاد with چین 'folds/pleating' as the manner of adornment—literally 'placed with ritual and adorned with folds'. The repeated verb Nهاد is typical poetic parallelism.

sentences.s076-s13: The champion became so joyful at that speech that, in the company of the old man, he was made young again.
sentences.s076-s14: He quickly dispatched a rider to Kabul; he asked Mehrab, “Where had he gone?”
sentences.s076-s15: To please the sovereign of the world, and from that joy which departed from the guests.
sentences.s076-s16: Now, when the tales reach me, I will present both in the manner that befits.
sentences.s076-s17: The king of Kabulistan became so joyful because of the union with Khorshid of Zabulistan.
sentences.s076-s18: As it was said, they cast away their lives; from every place the peacemakers sang/called.
sentences.s076-s19: When Mehrab became joyful and light‑spirited, his lips turned to a smile and his heart was glad.
sentences.s076-s20: He summoned the noble Sindukht before him; with very eloquent words he addressed her.
sentences.s076-s9: I sent him off with a joyful heart; may ill suspicion be far from him.
sentences.s076-s10: Out came — with Farrokh and Zal — the gold; heads rose from the ranks of the host.
sentences.s076-s11: They struck a navand (a musical strain) near Sam, for I had returned from the king with a glad heart.
sentences.s076-s12: He refused the royal robe of honor and the crown, that same yāreh and the collar, and even the ivory throne.
sentences.s076-s41: The whole of Kabulistan became adorned, full of color and scent and full of longing.
sentences.s076-s42: Everyone adorned the elephants' backs; from Kabul the worshippers made requests.
sentences.s076-s43: The entertainers sat upon the elephants, having placed golden helmets on their heads.
sentences.s076-s44: They prepared the reception; everyone wished to offer him musk and gold.
sentences.s076-s25: When Sindukht heard it, she turned back from him and approached the maiden — the teller of the secret.
sentences.s076-s26: She repeatedly gave her the good news of meeting Zal, saying, 'Have you seen anyone such as could be his equal?'
sentences.s076-s27: For woman and man of lofty origin, it is fitting that the worthy one raise his head from reproach.
sentences.s076-s28: You hastened swiftly toward the heart's desire; now whatever you sought, you have all obtained.
sentences.s076-s1: Then Sam wrote a reply to that letter, and he penned wondrous, felicitous words.
sentences.s076-s2: O famed, brave champion — victorious in every enterprise, bring (or may you bring) the lion.
sentences.s076-s3: The turning heavens do not behold another like you — in battle and in the feast, in counsel and in appearance.
sentences.s076-s4: That very son — the auspicious son, Zal's horseman — from whom a memorial remains in the world.
sentences.s076-s5: He arrived, and I understood from his desire that it was that very wish, counsel, and calm of his.
sentences.s076-s6: Whatever it was that you desired came to pass; that same counsel and calm were Zal's.
sentences.s076-s7: I entrusted all my wishes to him; I counted many happy days for him.
sentences.s076-s8: From a lion whose quarry is a leopard, what can be born except, from such a lion, one fierce in battle?
sentences.s076-s37: Her entire body was laden with gems; among the jewels, designs had been carved.
sentences.s076-s38: The pedestal of the throne was of ruby for me; it was the throne of the Kians and richly endowed.
sentences.s076-s39: There was an iwan where all kinds of robes and wine were brought from Pars, Ahvaz, and Rey.
sentences.s076-s40: He adorned Rudabeh like a beloved/beauty, full of garments, color, and the scent of spring.
sentences.s076-s21: He said to her, "O pair of auspicious counsels, he would not part with this gloomy place from his standard."
sentences.s076-s22: You struck (it) with your hand at a branch that was in the ground; go — kings will give acclamation.
sentences.s076-s23: How and where did you make it so from the first? In the end he sought that this should come to me.
sentences.s076-s24: All the treasure is set before you; if there is an ivory throne, if you wish.
sentences.s076-s29: Rudabeh, the royal lady, said to him, “You are worthy of praise in every assembly.”
sentences.s076-s30: I will make the dust of your feet my pillow; at your command I will arrange the rites of religion.
sentences.s076-s31: May the eyes of devils be far from you; may your heart and life be a house of feasting.
sentences.s076-s32: When Sindokht heard his words, she turned her face to the adornment of the palace.
sentences.s076-s33: She adorned the halls like paradise, fashioning rosewater, fruits, musk, and amber.
sentences.s076-s34: She spread out a carpet and set the figure in gold, with topaz woven all the way through.
sentences.s076-s35: Another part of her body lay in sweet water, so that each bead was a drop of water.
sentences.s076-s36: In one iwan she placed a whole golden throne, and (she) set it up with ceremony and adorned it with pleats/folds.
